perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall come into force on the date both parties have executed this Agreement. All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within six (6) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the date of the last disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall come into force on the date both parties have executed this Agreement. All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within six (6) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the fixed 5-year term and replaces it with 'indefinitely.' This creates a contradiction because later, the agreement mentions returning documents, which implicitly assumes a finite term. An indefinite term also makes the return of documents less clear as to when that should occur.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient shall remain the property of Disclosor. The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a 5-year term, which aligns with the requirement to return documents after the agreement's term. The changed text replaces the 5-year term with 'indefinitely,' which contradicts the clause requiring the return of documents. If the agreement lasts indefinitely, the timing of the document return becomes unclear, creating a direct conflict and inconsistency because the return of documents implies a finite duration.

perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed to include any technical, marketing, business and trade secret information disclosed by the Disclosor, but excludes such information which: (a)is presently in Recipient's possession, provided that: (i) such information has not been obtained from the Disclosor; (ii) that such possession can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records and (iii) that Recipient so informs Disclosor in writing within 30 days from receipt of such Confidential Information; (b)is, or becomes, generally available to the public through, for example, such sources as patents or other generally circulated publications, and such availability to the public does not result from any fault of the Recipient; (c)is received by the Recipient in written form from a third party having no obligation to the Disclosor to keep it confidential; or (d)is independently developed by the Recipient provided that such development can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed to include any information disclosed by the Disclosor.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Confidential Information' removes all the exclusions (a, b, c, d). This means information previously excluded, such as information already in the Recipient's possession or publicly available, is now considered confidential. This contradicts the implied understanding that only truly confidential information needs protection and creates burden on the recipient.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient agrees that the sole purpose for the Disclosor disclosing its Confidential Information to the Recipient is to enable Recipient to evaluate the Confidential Information for purposes of the Discussion. The Recipient will take all reasonable steps necessary to hold in strictest confidence all Confidential Information and to use such Confidential Information solely for this purpose. Any use by the Recipient of such Confidential Information for any other purpose shall be considered unauthorized.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes all exclusions from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text', which states the recipient will hold in strictest confidence 'all Confidential Information' and use it solely for the purpose of the discussion. Previously, the recipient wasn't obligated to treat information already in their possession as confidential. Now, they are, creating a significant inconsistency and burden.

perturbation.original_text: Where practical, Confidential Information shall be disclosed in Documentary or tangible form marked "Confidential". Where disclosures are made orally or by visual inspection, the Disclosor shall confirm in writing the fact and general nature of each disclosure within a reasonable time after each such disclosure is made.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall be disclosed at the Discloser's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause establishes a procedure for documenting confidential disclosures, either in writing or followed up with written confirmation. The modified clause eliminates this requirement, giving the Discloser sole discretion. This creates a contradiction because the rest of the agreement assumes there is a record of what was disclosed as confidential information, e.g., for proving a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient shall remain the property of Disclosor. The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified a procedure for documenting confidential disclosures, ensuring a record exists. The changed text gives the Discloser sole discretion on whether to document them, eliminating the requirement. The contradicted text states that all confidential information remains the property of the Discloser and should be returned upon request. Without a record of what was disclosed as confidential information, the Recipient may not know what documents or materials 'pertain to the Discussion' and therefore would need to be returned, thus contradicting the Recipient's responsibility to return such tangible material.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees that the sole purpose for the Disclosor disclosing its Confidential Information to the Recipient is to enable Recipient to evaluate the Confidential Information for purposes of the Discussion. The Recipient will take all reasonable steps necessary to hold in strictest confidence all Confidential Information and to use such Confidential Information solely for this purpose. Any use by the Recipient of such Confidential Information for any other purpose shall be considered unauthorized.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees that the sole purpose for the Disclosor disclosing its Confidential Information to the Recipient is to enable Recipient to evaluate the Confidential Information for purposes of the Discussion.
perturbation.explanation: The modified clause removes the requirement for the Recipient to take 'all reasonable steps necessary to hold in strictest confidence'. While the purpose remains evaluation, the explicit duty of confidentiality is weakened. This is a contradiction because the rest of the agreement relies on the Recipient actively protecting the information and potentially conflicts with clause 6.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the recipient to take 'all reasonable steps necessary to hold in strictest confidence' the confidential information. The changed text removes this explicit obligation. The contradicted text states that the Recipient agrees that it will not disclose confidential information through any act or omission. By removing the explicit duty to protect confidentiality, the changed text contradicts the contradicted text. The changed text makes it possible for the Recipient to disclose confidential information through omission since there is no longer an explicit obligation to actively prevent disclosure, as required in the original text and assumed by the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will disclose Confidential Information only to its employees, agents and representatives with a need to know such Confidential Information who are bound in writing to keep it confidential and only to the extent necessary for the Discussion to be conducted.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient may disclose Confidential Information to its employees, agents and representatives.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the 'need to know,' 'bound in writing,' and 'only to the extent necessary' limitations on disclosure. The original text requires that individuals receiving the information must have a genuine need for it, be contractually obligated to maintain its confidentiality, and only receive as much information as necessary. The modified text loosens restrictions on information dissemination, and creates a contradiction with clause 6 which prevents unauthorized disclousre.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.justification: The modified text eliminates the 'need to know,' 'bound in writing,' and 'only to the extent necessary' restrictions on disclosing confidential information. The original text imposed these limitations to prevent unauthorized disclosure. The contradicted text states that the recipient will not disclose Confidential Information to others through any act or omission, except with prior written consent. Because the modified text loosens restrictions on information dissemination and allows the Recipient to disclose Confidential Information to employees, agents and representatives without limitation, it directly contradicts the contradicted text which prevents unauthorized disclosure, thus there is a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient,' the Recipient is no longer explicitly prohibited from disclosing the confidential information to third parties. This contradicts the fundamental purpose of the NDA, which is to prevent unauthorized disclosure. This omission creates an internal contradiction because the agreement's entire premise is the protection of confidential information from disclosure, and now this is not explicitly prevented.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient agrees that the sole purpose for the Disclosor disclosing its Confidential Information to the Recipient is to enable Recipient to evaluate the Confidential Information for purposes of the Discussion. The Recipient will take all reasonable steps necessary to hold in strictest confidence all Confidential Information and to use such Confidential Information solely for this purpose. Any use by the Recipient of such Confidential Information for any other purpose shall be considered unauthorized.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibited the Recipient from disclosing Confidential Information to others through any act or omission. The changed text removes this explicit prohibition, only restricting the Recipient from using the Confidential Information without consent. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which states that the Recipient will hold all Confidential Information in strictest confidence and use it solely for the purpose of evaluation, implying a duty to prevent disclosure to third parties. The omission in the changed text now allows for the possibility of disclosure, contradicting the intention to keep the information confidential.
